subject: Cardiology
title: The cerebral cortex, meninges, basal ganglia, and ventricular system
preambleMarkdown:
# The cerebral cortex, meninges, basal ganglia, and ventricular system

> **Clinical-review notice:** This is an examination-focused draft. Local protocols, medication choices, procedure thresholds, anticoagulation decisions, pregnancy/pediatric care, and device or surgical indications must be checked against current institutional and national guidance before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown: - The cerebral cortex, meninges, basal ganglia, and ventricular system is a foundational anatomy and physiology topic used to connect structure with clinical disease. Exam questions emphasize location, blood supply, functional organization, lesion patterns, and how anatomy explains symptoms and imaging findings.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This topic is anatomically neurovascular rather than cardiac, but is retained in the indexed cardiology folder.
- Clinical value comes from localization of cortical, basal ganglia, meningeal, and ventricular lesions.
- Blood supply and CSF anatomy explain stroke and hydrocephalus patterns.
- Urgent neurologic deficits require emergency stroke evaluation.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown: - The cerebral cortex, meninges, basal ganglia, and ventricular system refers to the named anatomic structures and their clinically relevant functions, vascular supply, innervation, and relationships.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Gross anatomy.
- Microscopic or tissue organization.
- Vascular supply and venous drainage.
- Functional regions.
- Common clinical lesion patterns.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Anatomic topics are high yield because common clinical syndromes follow predictable blood supply, innervation, conduction, drainage, and spatial relationships. Exams test localization more than prevalence.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Describe the named structure by borders, relations, blood supply, venous drainage, lymphatic drainage, innervation, microscopic organization when relevant, and nearby structures that explain complications.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Function follows structure: valves direct flow, conduction tissue coordinates contraction, vessels distribute oxygen, meninges protect the brain, ventricles circulate CSF, and basal ganglia modulate movement.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Disease occurs when structure is compressed, inflamed, ischemic, malformed, degenerated, ruptured, obstructed, or disconnected from its normal functional pathway.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Causes include congenital malformation, trauma, ischemia, infection, inflammation, degeneration, neoplasm, iatrogenic injury, and systemic disease affecting the structure.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Risk factors depend on the structure but commonly include age, trauma, vascular risk, congenital disease, infection exposure, autoimmune disease, family history, and prior procedures.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Genetic contribution is most important when presentation is early, familial, syndromic, associated with sudden death, or linked to cardiomyopathy, channelopathy, aortopathy, or congenital heart disease. First-degree relatives may need screening when an inherited syndrome is suspected.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Microbiology is usually not the primary driver unless infection causes endocarditis, myocarditis, pericarditis, infected aneurysm, sepsis-related cardiovascular collapse, prosthetic-device infection, or vascular graft infection. Fever, bacteremia, immunosuppression, injection drug use, or recent procedures should prompt an infectious evaluation.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms depend on the structure involved, acuity, and vascular territory.
- Lesions may present with pain, weakness, sensory loss, rhythm disturbance, murmur, or imaging abnormality depending on the topic.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Ask about onset, duration, triggers, exertional component, positional or pleuritic features, syncope, dyspnea, palpitations, chest pain, neurologic symptoms, bleeding, fever, medication use, stimulant use, pregnancy, recent procedures, prior ECG or imaging, prior cardiovascular disease, and family history of sudden death or inherited disease.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Assess vital signs, oxygenation, mental status, perfusion, volume status, pulses, jugular venous pressure, precordial impulse, heart sounds, murmurs, lungs, edema, neurologic deficits, skin findings, and signs of shock. Compare bilateral pulses and blood pressures when aortic or vascular disease is possible.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute neurologic deficit.
- Syncope with exertion.
- Chest pain with shock.
- New focal weakness or aphasia.
- Rapidly progressive structural symptoms.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Localization-based differentials compare neighboring structures, vascular territories, nerve distributions, referred pain patterns, and common mimics.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use history and examination to localize first.
- Select ECG, ultrasound, CT, MRI, angiography, or electrophysiology studies based on the suspected structure.
- Correlate imaging with functional deficits.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Use examination localization first, then select ECG, ultrasound, CT, MRI, angiography, electrophysiology, or laboratory testing based on the structure and urgency.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Diagnosis should combine symptoms, examination, ECG or rhythm evidence, imaging, biomarkers, and accepted disease-specific criteria. Avoid diagnosing from one abnormal test in isolation when the clinical syndrome does not fit.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Severity is based on hemodynamic stability, symptom burden, organ dysfunction, ECG or imaging risk features, biomarker pattern, ventricular function, lesion severity, arrhythmia risk, embolic risk, bleeding risk, frailty, and need for urgent procedural intervention.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Initial Management

- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, disability, and exposure. Place unstable patients on monitoring, obtain IV access, treat hypoxemia, correct immediately reversible causes, obtain an ECG, and escalate early to cardiology, emergency medicine, ICU, vascular surgery, electrophysiology, or cardiac surgery when high-risk features are present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, smoking cessation, exercise or cardiac rehabilitation when appropriate, diet quality, weight management, sleep apnea treatment, alcohol or stimulant reduction, vaccination when relevant, and shared decision-making. Activity restrictions depend on acuity, symptoms, rhythm risk, structural disease, and procedure plans.

### Pharmacological Management

- Medication choices depend on the exact syndrome and contraindications. Common classes include antiplatelets, anticoagulants, statins, antihypertensives, beta blockers, calcium-channel blockers, nitrates, diuretics, guideline-directed heart-failure therapies, antiarrhythmics, antibiotics, or analgesics. Dose details require local protocol and patient-specific renal, hepatic, pregnancy, bleeding, and interaction review.

### Procedures

- Procedural options may include cardioversion, defibrillation, temporary or permanent pacing, catheter ablation, coronary angiography or PCI, CABG, valve intervention, vascular repair, thrombectomy, thrombolysis, pericardiocentesis, endovascular repair, or diagnostic catheterization depending on the topic.

### Surgical Management

- Surgery or structural intervention is considered for unstable anatomy, refractory symptoms despite medical therapy, high-risk coronary or valvular disease, congenital lesions, aortic disease, vascular complications, device infection, or complications such as mechanical rupture. Timing depends on acuity, anatomy, procedural risk, expected durability, and patient goals.

- Treat emergencies first, then address the underlying anatomic lesion.
- Use specialist referral when structural intervention, congenital disease, neurovascular compromise, or procedural planning is required.
- Use rehabilitation and prevention when anatomy-related injury causes chronic impairment.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Monitor symptoms, vital signs, rhythm, perfusion, urine output when ill, medication adverse effects, renal function and electrolytes when using diuretics or renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system therapy, anticoagulation safety, device function when present, and disease-specific imaging intervals.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Follow-up should confirm diagnosis, review response to therapy, reconcile medications, address risk factors, arrange rehabilitation or specialist care, document warning symptoms, and ensure surveillance for recurrence, progression, procedural complications, or adverse drug effects.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Complications may include shock, heart failure, pulmonary edema, myocardial infarction, stroke, systemic embolism, sudden cardiac death, malignant arrhythmia, bleeding from therapy, renal injury, infection, recurrent hospitalization, procedure-related injury, and chronic functional limitation.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prognosis depends on acuity, ventricular function, comorbid disease, age, kidney function, adherence, response to therapy, reversibility of the cause, and access to timely emergency or procedural care. Early recognition and appropriate follow-up reduce preventable morbidity.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Prevention includes smoking cessation, blood-pressure control, lipid management, diabetes care, physical activity, diet quality, weight management, treatment of sleep apnea, avoidance of cardiotoxic exposures, vaccination when relevant, and family screening for inherited disease when indicated.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Explain the diagnosis, uncertainty that remains, warning symptoms, medication purpose and adverse effects, follow-up plan, activity guidance, and when to seek urgent care. Use plain language and confirm understanding.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Trend vital signs, oxygenation, pain, mental status, perfusion, rhythm, intake/output when ill, and response to treatment.
- Escalate promptly for red flags, new neurologic symptoms, recurrent chest pain, hypotension, syncope, worsening dyspnea, or malignant rhythm.
- Reinforce medication adherence, follow-up, lifestyle changes, and procedure instructions.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy status when relevant, interactions, duplicate anticoagulant or antiplatelet therapy, QT-prolonging drugs, and adherence barriers.
- High-risk medications require indication, duration, monitoring plan, and bleeding or toxicity counseling.
- Reconcile medications at transitions of care.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Most cardiology conditions do not automatically require antibiotic prophylaxis for dental care. Consider endocarditis prophylaxis only for guideline-defined high-risk cardiac conditions and procedures, and coordinate anticoagulant management for invasive dental procedures when needed.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### Pediatrics

- Pediatric presentations may reflect congenital disease, inherited arrhythmia, myocarditis, Kawasaki disease, multisystem inflammatory syndromes, or rare familial vascular disease. Use age-specific vital signs, doses, and pediatric specialist pathways.

### Pregnancy

- Pregnancy changes heart rate, plasma volume, coagulation, and imaging or medication choices. Maternal stabilization comes first in emergencies. Coordinate cardiology, obstetrics, anesthesia, and neonatology for significant disease.

### Older Adults

- Older adults often have atypical symptoms, polypharmacy, frailty, kidney disease, cognitive impairment, and higher bleeding or procedural risk. Goals of care and functional status should shape decisions.

### Renal Impairment

- Renal disease changes biomarker interpretation, medication dosing, contrast risk, electrolyte vulnerability, and bleeding risk. Avoid delaying life-saving imaging or intervention when the emergency risk is greater than contrast risk.

### Hepatic Impairment

- Assess coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, drug metabolism, volume status, and procedural bleeding risk. Medication selection and anticoagulation decisions require individualized review.

### Immunocompromised Patients

- Maintain a lower threshold for infection, atypical presentations, drug interactions, and device-related complications. Coordinate antimicrobial and procedural decisions with specialists when infection is possible.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Major North American and European guidance generally agrees on stabilizing emergencies first, using ECG and echocardiography appropriately, treating modifiable risk factors aggressively, and matching procedures to anatomy and risk. Differences commonly involve thresholds, timing, scoring systems, medication preferences, and local resource pathways. Use current local guidance for patient care.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stability determines the first action.
- Compare current ECG, imaging, and symptoms with prior data whenever possible.
- Treat the patient, not a single number.
- Reassess after each intervention.
- Imported notes below are provenance; current synthesis should be reviewed against up-to-date guidelines.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Delaying emergency treatment for nonessential testing.
- Ignoring medication contraindications, renal function, pregnancy, or bleeding risk.
- Assuming a normal initial test excludes a dynamic cardiovascular process.
- Failing to arrange follow-up after an ED or inpatient diagnosis.
- Removing imported source notes instead of preserving provenance.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
### USMLE Step 1

- Focus on anatomy, physiology, pathology, pharmacology, ECG mechanisms, hemodynamics, and classic disease associations.

### USMLE Step 2 CK

- Prioritize presentation, next best diagnostic test, initial stabilization, red flags, and first-line management.

### USMLE Step 3

- Emphasize sequencing: stabilize first, choose the right unit of care, monitor treatment response, prevent complications, and plan discharge follow-up.

### ABIM

- Expect guideline nuance, comorbid disease, renal impairment, anticoagulation decisions, procedural indications, and treatment failure.

### ABFM

- Focus on prevention, screening when applicable, risk-factor control, medication adherence, counseling, and recognizing emergencies that need transfer.

### Surgery boards

- Know when vascular, cardiac, or structural intervention is urgent; understand anatomy, perioperative risk, and complications.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- MCA stroke affects face/arm more than leg.
- ACA stroke affects leg more than arm.
- Basal ganglia lesions can cause movement disorders.
- Meningeal irritation causes headache, fever, neck stiffness, or photophobia.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown: - Areas requiring ongoing review include individualized thresholds for intervention, risk prediction in underrepresented groups, long-term comparative effectiveness of procedures and devices, optimal antithrombotic duration, and implementation of guideline-directed therapy in patients with multimorbidity.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI. 2025 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2025-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-acute-coronary-syndromes
- AHA/ACC/HFSA. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-management-of-heart-failure
- ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS. 2023 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation. https://www.ahajournals.org/doi/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001193
- ACC/AHA. 2020 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2020-acc-aha-guideline-for-the-management-of-patients-with-valvular-heart-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2022 Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease. https://professional.heart.org/en/science-news/2022-guideline-for-the-diagnosis-and-management-of-aortic-disease
- ACC/AHA. 2024 Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease. https://professional.heart.org/-/media/PHD-Files-2/Science-News/2/2024/2024-PAD-guideline-slide-set.pdf
